beats.text: Meet the complex vector one, i — sitting in complex space.
Try the usual length formula: one squared plus i squared. That's one minus one — zero. A nonzero vector with zero length. Something's broken.

The fix: conjugate before multiplying. One times one, plus i times negative i. That gives one plus one — length root two. The conjugate transpose, written with a superscript H, replaces the regular transpose here.
A matrix that equals its own conjugate transpose is Hermitian. A highlight surrounds the entries that mirror across the diagonal — each one conjugated.
Hermitian matrices guarantee real eigenvalues. Two eigenvector arrows appear, orthogonal under the conjugate inner product — no complex weirdness, just clean perpendicular geometry.
Conjugate the transpose, and all of linear algebra extends into complex space. Seamlessly.
